title: About
text: use pilotr published work, please cite . Bernabeu, P. (2026). pilotr: Simulate experimental behavioural data portable design specification (R Python package version 0.1.0). https://github.com/pablobernabeu/pilotr Download BibTeX entry R, citation("pilotr") returns reference.
code: @software{bernabeu2026pilotr,   author  = {Pablo Bernabeu},   title   = {{pilotr}: Simulate Experimental and Behavioural Data from a Portable Design Specification},   year    = {2026},   version = {0.1.0},   url     = {https://github.com/pablobernabeu/pilotr} }

title: About
text: pilotr developed Pablo Bernabeu, researcher Department Education University Oxford. work spans cognitive psychology, neuroscience, linguistics, education research methods, hands-experience behavioural experiments, EEG, corpus analysis, computational modelling statistics. develops open, reproducible research software R Python, Fellow Software Sustainability Institute.

title: Cross-language reproducibility
text: toolkit spans R Python faces subtle obstacle. two ecosystems use different default random-number generators (R’s Mersenne-Twister inversion, NumPy’s PCG64), direct port produces different data seed. undermine guarantee design authored can reproduced anywhere.

title: Cross-language reproducibility
text: pilotr sets aside native RNGs provides shared generator implemented identically languages. Uniform deviates come L’Ecuyer’s (1988) combined linear congruential generator, whose arithmetic stays 2532^{53} therefore exact IEEE-754 doubles (R) Python integers alike. Normal deviates use Wichura’s (1988) Algorithm 241 inverse-CDF, algorithm R’s qnorm() uses, deviates agree full double precision. remaining draws (Cholesky-correlated random effects, inverse-CDF Poisson ordinal draws, Marsaglia–Tsang gamma draws Beta family, Fisher–Yates item sampler partial crossing) derived two documented, identical consumption order. result, given specification seed, R Python implementations produce bit-identical data sets. specification, rather implementation, serves source truth.

title: Cross-language reproducibility
text: specification seed always give data. Changing seed changes draws, structure ground-truth parameters stay fixed.
code: spec <- build_spec(list(   name = "demo", seed = 2024, design_kind = "between", n_subject = 200,   factor_name = "group", lev1 = "a", lev2 = "b",   intercept = 100, effect = 5, family = "gaussian", resp_name = "score", sigma = 10))  isTRUE(all.equal(simulate_design(spec), simulate_design(spec))) #> [1] TRUE spec2 <- spec; spec2$seed <- spec$seed + 1L identical(simulate_design(spec)$score, simulate_design(spec2)$score) #> [1] FALSE

title: Cross-language reproducibility
text: Python package reads identical specification produces identical data. code evaluated . shows Python run, output matches R data last bit. repository includes parity check simulates designs languages reports maximum absolute difference. difference 0.0 across thousands rows, categorical mismatches. simulation core also bit-identical across machines R versions, design verified laptop reproduces exactly HPC node.
code: from pilotr import simulate, load_spec  # the same design.json the R package (and the app) use data = simulate("design.json")

title: Cross-language reproducibility
text: specification source truth generator shared, design can authored (app, R Python) reproduced everywhere. can preregistered small JSON file anyone can run, scaled laptop cluster without drift, handed collaborators work different languages. property turns informal request simulate data reproducible, shareable methodological artefact.

title: Getting started with pilotr
text: pilotr built around portable design specification fully describes data-generating process consumed identically three interfaces, namely -code web application, R package Python package. specification unit reproducibility. complete, executable description rather screenshot graphical interface language-specific script. can place version control, attach preregistration, share collaborator, run unchanged R Python obtain bit-identical data. vignette walks core loop describe, simulate, inspect export. vignettes go deeper power, precision/ROPE design analysis, cross-language reproducibility, response families.

title: Getting started with pilotr
text: specification small list serialised JSON. can write hand, point click web application, build flat set inputs build_spec(). example crossed psycholinguistic design. Subjects respond items related unrelated condition, reaction time outcome, crossed -subject -item random intercepts slopes, small priming effect log scale. portable artefact plain JSON. exactly web application downloads Python package reads. can read specification back file (string) load_spec(), design authored anywhere runs everywhere.
code: spec <- build_spec(list(   name = "priming", seed = 2024,   design_kind = "within", include_items = TRUE,   n_subject = 24, n_item = 20,   factor_name = "condition", lev1 = "related", lev2 = "unrelated",   intercept = 6.0, effect = 0.05,   subj_int_sd = 0.12, subj_slope_sd = 0.04, subj_corr = 0.2,   item_int_sd = 0.08, item_slope_sd = 0.02, item_corr = -0.1,   family = "shifted_lognormal", resp_name = "RT", sigma = 0.30, shift = 200 )) cat(spec_json(spec)) #> { #>   "name": "priming", #>   "seed": 2024, #>   "units": { #>     "subject": { #>       "n": 24 #>     }, #>     "item": { #>       "n": 20 #>     } #>   }, #>   "factors": [ #>     { #>       "name": "condition", #>       "levels": ["related", "unrelated"], #>       "contrasts": { #>         "effect": [-0.5, 0.5] #>       }, #>       "vary_within": ["subject", "item"] #>     } #>   ], #>   "fixed": { #>     "intercept": 6, #>     "coefficients": { #>       "effect": 0.05 #>     } #>   }, #>   "random": { #>     "subject": { #>       "intercept_sd": 0.12, #>       "slopes": { #>         "effect": 0.04 #>       }, #>       "correlations": { #>         "intercept,effect": 0.2 #>       } #>     }, #>     "item": { #>       "intercept_sd": 0.08, #>       "slopes": { #>         "effect": 0.02 #>       }, #>       "correlations": { #>         "intercept,effect": -0.1 #>       } #>     } #>   }, #>   "response": { #>     "family": "shifted_lognormal", #>     "name": "RT", #>     "sigma": 0.3, #>     "round": 4, #>     "shift": 200 #>   } #> }

title: Getting started with pilotr
text: simulate_design() turns specification, supplied either list path JSON file, analysis-ready data frame. design crossed, every subject sees every item conditions (960 rows ).
code: data <- simulate_design(spec) nrow(data) #> [1] 960 head(data) #>   subject item condition       RT #> 1       1    1   related 431.0559 #> 2       1    1 unrelated 488.0423 #> 3       1    2   related 665.4629 #> 4       1    2 unrelated 771.0603 #> 5       1    3   related 480.7395 #> 6       1    3 unrelated 877.9173

title: Getting started with pilotr
text: pilotr ships shared random-number generator, specification seed produce data every time. holds current R session, fresh one, Python, machine. Changing seed changes data, leaving specification unchanged leaves data unchanged. cross-language vignette describes holds across R Python.
code: isTRUE(all.equal(simulate_design(spec), simulate_design(spec))) #> [1] TRUE

title: Getting started with pilotr
text: generate_r_script() emits stand-alone R script embeds specification reproduces design external files. convenient appendix preregistration.
code: cat(generate_r_script(spec)) #> # Reproducible simulation exported by pilotr. #> # install.packages("pilotr")   # once available; then run this script as-is. #> library(pilotr) #>  #> spec <- list(name = "priming", seed = 2024L, units = list(subject = list( #>     n = 24L), item = list(n = 20L)), factors = list(list(name = "condition",  #>     levels = c("related", "unrelated"), contrasts = list(effect = c(-0.5,  #>     0.5)), vary_within = c("subject", "item"))), fixed = list( #>     intercept = 6, coefficients = list(effect = 0.05)), random = list( #>     subject = list(intercept_sd = 0.12, slopes = list(effect = 0.04),  #>         correlations = list("intercept,effect" = 0.2)), item = list( #>         intercept_sd = 0.08, slopes = list(effect = 0.02), correlations = list( #>             "intercept,effect" = -0.1))), response = list(family = "shifted_lognormal",  #>     name = "RT", sigma = 0.3, round = 4L, shift = 200)) #>  #> data <- simulate_design(spec)              # analysis-ready data frame #> # write.csv(data, "data.csv", row.names = FALSE) #> # pow  <- power_mixed(spec, n_sims = 200)   # simulation-based power + Type S/M

title: Getting started with pilotr
text: Power design analysis, covering simulation-based power Type S Type M errors, two-group crossed mixed-effects designs. Precision / ROPE design analysis, plans precision region practical equivalence sweeps sample size. Cross-language reproducibility, describing shared generator makes R Python agree last bit. Response families, covering Gaussian, reaction-time, accuracy, count, ordinal proportion outcomes.

title: Power and design analysis
text: classic case closed-form analytic power, simulation matches. roughly 50% power, Type M ratio well 1. Conditional significance, estimated effect exaggerated, even though average estimate replicates unbiased. reflects statistical-significance filter, precisely design analysis meant expose.
code: spec <- build_spec(list(   name = "two_group", seed = 1, design_kind = "between", n_subject = 64,   factor_name = "group", lev1 = "control", lev2 = "treatment",   intercept = 100, effect = 5, family = "gaussian", resp_name = "score", sigma = 10))  pw <- power_design(spec, n_sims = 500) unlist(pw[c("power", "type_s", "type_m", "true_effect", "mean_estimate")]) #>         power        type_s        type_m   true_effect mean_estimate  #>      0.504000      0.000000      1.372571      5.000000      4.947259

title: Power and design analysis
text: feature distinguishes pilotr marginal simulator power estimation crossed -subject -item designs. R interface fits maximal model y ~ cond + (1 + cond | subject) + (1 + cond | item) lme4/lmerTest tests fixed effect Satterthwaite degrees freedom. n_converged reports many replicates maximal model actually fit. useful diagnostic right, since convergence problems common small crossed designs.
code: spec_c <- build_spec(list(   name = "priming", seed = 1, design_kind = "within", include_items = TRUE,   n_subject = 24, n_item = 18,   factor_name = "condition", lev1 = "related", lev2 = "unrelated",   intercept = 6, effect = 0.05,   subj_int_sd = 0.12, subj_slope_sd = 0.04, subj_corr = 0.2,   item_int_sd = 0.08, item_slope_sd = 0.02, item_corr = -0.1,   family = "shifted_lognormal", resp_name = "RT", sigma = 0.3, shift = 200))  pm <- power_mixed(spec_c, n_sims = 20)   # tiny for the vignette; use >= 200 in practice unlist(pm[c("power", "type_s", "type_m", "n_converged")]) #>       power      type_s      type_m n_converged  #>    0.450000    0.000000    1.494293   20.000000

title: Power and design analysis
text: following sweep number subjects shows power crosses target.  high-resolution sweep, analyses fully parallel scale well cluster. repository includes SLURM array job (hpc/precision_array.slurm) runs precision sweep across many cores.
code: curve <- power_curve_mixed(spec_c, subject_ns = c(15, 30), n_sims = 12) curve #>   n_subject     power   type_m #> 1        15 0.2500000 1.843531 #> 2        30 0.5833333 1.239214  library(ggplot2) ggplot(curve, aes(n_subject, power)) +   geom_hline(yintercept = 0.8, linetype = 2, colour = "grey60") +   geom_line(colour = "#2C6FB0", linewidth = 0.8) +   geom_point(colour = "#2C6FB0", size = 2.6) +   scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, 1)) +   labs(x = expression(italic(N) ~ "subjects"), y = "Power") +   theme_minimal(base_size = 12)

what: A bridge to the Bayesian workflow
title: Power and design analysis
text: confirmatory Bayesian fit, brms_bridge() emits ready--run brms model. provides family, fixed random-effects formula, weakly-informative prior set, derived specification, planning model confirmatory model remain consistent.
code: brms_bridge(spec_c) #> library(brms) #> fit <- brm( #>   RT ~ effect + (1 + effect | subject) + (1 + effect | item), #>   data   = your_data, #>   family = shifted_lognormal(), #>   prior  = c( #>     prior(normal(0, 2.5), class = "Intercept"), #>     prior(normal(0, 0.5), class = "b", coef = "effect"), #>     prior(normal(0, 1), class = "sd"), #>     prior(lkj(2), class = "cor") #>   ), #>   chains = 4, iter = 4000, warmup = 2000, cores = 4, #>   control = list(adapt_delta = 0.95) #> )

title: Power and design analysis
text: precision / ROPE vignette covers design analysis question concerns practical significance instead statistical significance.

title: Precision and ROPE design analysis
text: Monte Carlo replicates, focal fixed effect, pilotr records 95% confidence interval whether falls determinately outside ROPE (effect practically meaningful) entirely inside (practical equivalence zero), together expected interval width. Sweeping sample size locates minimum N focal effect reaches determinate decision target probability.

title: Precision and ROPE design analysis
text: reuse crossed priming design, represents small priming effect log reaction time. analysis can run specification alone, pilotr auto-derives analysis model, including contrast columns, response transform mixed-model formula.
code: spec_c <- build_spec(list(   name = "priming", seed = 1, design_kind = "within", include_items = TRUE,   n_subject = 24, n_item = 18,   factor_name = "condition", lev1 = "related", lev2 = "unrelated",   intercept = 6, effect = 0.05,   subj_int_sd = 0.12, subj_slope_sd = 0.04, subj_corr = 0.2,   item_int_sd = 0.08, item_slope_sd = 0.02, item_corr = -0.1,   family = "shifted_lognormal", resp_name = "RT", sigma = 0.3, shift = 200)) model_formula(spec_c) #> .y ~ effect + (1 + effect | subject) + (1 + effect | item) #> <environment: 0x55b3252b2c38>

title: Precision and ROPE design analysis
text: declare focal effect (coefficient name true value) ROPE half-width. effect lives log scale, treat anything smaller 0.02 practically equivalent zero. columns interpreted follows. p_meaningful probability 95% CI lands entirely outside ROPE, determinate ‘meaningful’ decision. p_equivalent probability lands entirely inside, determinate ‘equivalent’ decision. mean_ci_width expected precision.
code: pr <- precision_design(spec_c, focal = c(effect = 0.05), rope = 0.02, n_sims = 25) pr #>    param true mean_ci_width p_meaningful p_equivalent n_converged #> 1 effect 0.05    0.09491084          0.2            0          25

title: Precision and ROPE design analysis
text: N grows interval tightens, p_meaningful rises design reaches determinate decision reliably. curve can used choose smallest N meets target, example ‘p_meaningful ≥ 0.90’, provides informative criterion power point null.
code: prc <- precision_curve(spec_c, focal = c(effect = 0.05), subject_ns = c(15, 30), n_sims = 15) prc[, c("n_subject", "param", "p_meaningful", "p_equivalent", "mean_ci_width")] #>   n_subject  param p_meaningful p_equivalent mean_ci_width #> 1        15 effect   0.00000000            0    0.12283251 #> 2        30 effect   0.06666667            0    0.08389208

title: Precision and ROPE design analysis
text: power vignette covers simulation-based power Type S/M errors. getting-started vignette covers core simulate--inspect loop.

title: Response families
text: default, y=η+εy = \eta + \varepsilon residual standard deviation sigma. suits continuous, roughly symmetric outcomes ratings averaged many trials standardised scores.
code: g <- demo("gaussian", intercept = 100, effect = 5, sigma = 10) round(g$by_group, 2) #>   control treatment  #>     97.50    102.41 fam_hist(g$y, "#2C6FB0", "Gaussian", "score")

title: Response families
text: Reaction times right-skewed bounded . pilotr models y=shift+exp⁡(η+ε)y = \text{shift} + \exp(\eta + \varepsilon), effect log scale. intercept 6 implies typical RT near exp(6) ms shift.
code: rt <- demo("shifted_lognormal", intercept = 6, effect = 0.1, sigma = 0.3, shift = 200) round(rt$by_group, 1) #>   control treatment  #>     600.6     642.6 fam_hist(rt$y, "#B0402C", "Shifted lognormal (RT)", "RT (ms)")

title: Response families
text: Binary accuracy modelled logit link. intercept log-odds correct response, effect log-odds difference conditions.
code: acc <- demo("bernoulli", intercept = 0, effect = 0.5) round(acc$by_group, 3)   # P(correct) by group #>   control treatment  #>     0.446     0.569

title: Response families
text: Counts via log link (e.g. number fixations, errors, events). intercept 1.5 implies base rate near exp(1.5).
code: cts <- demo("poisson", intercept = 1.5, effect = 0.3) round(cts$by_group, 2)   # mean count by group #>   control treatment  #>      3.85      5.18 table(cts$y)[1:8] #>  #>   0   1   2   3   4   5   6   7  #>  41 225 466 698 711 659 466 334

title: Response families
text: Ordered categorical responses via cumulative-logit model user thresholds. effect shifts latent distribution across thresholds.
code: ord <- build_spec(list(   name = "likert", seed = 1, design_kind = "between", n_subject = 4000,   factor_name = "group", lev1 = "control", lev2 = "treatment",   intercept = 0, effect = 0.8, family = "ordinal", resp_name = "rating",   thresholds = "-2, -0.6, 0.6, 2")) r <- simulate_design(ord) round(prop.table(table(r$group, r$rating), 1), 2)   # category proportions by group #>             #>                1    2    3    4    5 #>   control   0.16 0.30 0.27 0.19 0.08 #>   treatment 0.09 0.18 0.29 0.28 0.17

title: Response families
text: Bounded proportions (0, 1) modelled mean–precision parameterisation. mean logit⁻¹(η) phi precision, larger values giving tighter distribution.
code: bt <- demo("beta", intercept = 0, effect = 0.8, phi = 8) round(bt$by_group, 3)   # mean proportion by group #>   control treatment  #>     0.400     0.592 fam_hist(bt$y, "#2E8B57", "Beta", "proportion")

title: Response families
text: match families researchers fit lme4, glmmTMB brms, design simulated corresponds model later fit. point--click application exposes six families. full engine, including continuous predictors, interactions, nesting partial crossing, available writing specification directly.

title: The no-code app
text: Installed, machine. package installed, launch app locally: user runs R process, heavy power run never blocks anyone else, simulations use local cores. browser, nothing install. serverless build runs entirely browser WebAssembly, server nothing uploaded. Try live demo. covers light path: build design, simulate, inspect data, estimate two-group Gaussian power export.
code: pilotr::run_app()

title: The no-code app
text: Describe design left panel: sample sizes, factor two levels, fixed intercept effect, response family, crossed designs random-effect standard deviations. read tabs. Design spec: exact JSON specification, portable source truth. Data: simulated data set. Summary & plot: group summaries plot. Power: simulation-based two-group Gaussian power Type S Type M errors, plus power curve sample size. R script: self-contained, reproducible R script. installed app can also verify running clean R session confirming bit--bit reproduction. Changing response family resets intercept effect sensible values family’s scale, point--click design stays valid. advanced ‘paste JSON spec’ box accepts designs beyond point--click controls, continuous predictors, interactions nesting.

title: The no-code app
text: app design; specification . Download specification (.json) data (.csv), run specification unchanged R simulate_design() Python pilotr.simulate() identical data. Crossed mixed-effects power (via lme4) precision/ROPE design analysis run installed packages rather browser. Whichever interface use, specification build app drives three.

what: Simulate Experimental and Behavioural Data from a Portable Design Specification
title: Simulate Experimental and Behavioural Data from a Portable Design Specification
text: Simulate experimental behavioural data portable design specification, integrated simulation-based power design analysis (Type S Type M errors, precision region practical equivalence). pilotr lets pilot study run . Describe design plan collect, groups, conditions, sample sizes, effect sizes outcome family, pilotr generates data design produce. can check study’s power design analysis gathering anything. single specification drives three interchangeable interfaces, namely -code web app, R package Python package; given specification seed, three produce bit-identical data.

title: Simulate Experimental and Behavioural Data from a Portable Design Specification
code: # install.packages("remotes") remotes::install_github("pablobernabeu/pilotr", subdir = "r/pilotr")

title: Simulate Experimental and Behavioural Data from a Portable Design Specification
code: library(pilotr)  spec <- load_spec("design.json")          # a pilotr JSON design spec data <- simulate_design(spec)             # analysis-ready data frame pow  <- power_mixed(spec, n_sims = 200)   # crossed-LMM power + Type S/M (lme4) cat(generate_r_script(spec))              # a self-contained, reproducible script

what: Inverse normal cumulative distribution function (Wichura's AS 241) — as241
title: Inverse normal cumulative distribution function (Wichura's AS 241) — as241
text: Compute standard-normal quantile probability using Wichura's (1988) Algorithm 241 (PPND16 routine), algorithm underlying [stats::qnorm()]. implemented identically R Python ports.

title: Inverse normal cumulative distribution function (Wichura's AS 241) — as241
Inverse normal cumulative distribution function (Wichura's AS 241) — as241
text: p probability open interval (0, 1).

title: Inverse normal cumulative distribution function (Wichura's AS 241) — as241
text: standard-normal quantile (numeric value) corresponding `p`.

title: Inverse normal cumulative distribution function (Wichura's AS 241) — as241

what: Derive a brms formula, family, and priors from a design spec — brms_bridge
title: Derive a brms formula, family, and priors from a design spec — brms_bridge
text: Derive brms formula, family, priors design spec

title: Derive a brms formula, family, and priors from a design spec — brms_bridge
code: brms_bridge(spec, prior_scale = 0.5, interaction_scale = NULL)

title: Derive a brms formula, family, and priors from a design spec — brms_bridge
text: spec design spec (path list). prior_scale SD Normal prior fixed main effects (standardised scale). interaction_scale SD Normal prior interaction terms (default prior_scale/2).

title: Derive a brms formula, family, and priors from a design spec — brms_bridge
text: Invisibly, list elements `formula`, `family`, `priors`, `code`;   `code` element (ready--fit `brms` model) also printed console.

title: Derive a brms formula, family, and priors from a design spec — brms_bridge
code: spec <- build_spec(list(name = "d", seed = 1, design_kind = "within",   include_items = TRUE, n_subject = 20, n_item = 12, factor_name = "cond",   lev1 = "a", lev2 = "b", intercept = 6, effect = 0.05,   subj_int_sd = 0.12, subj_slope_sd = 0.04, subj_corr = 0.2,   item_int_sd = 0.08, item_slope_sd = 0.02, item_corr = -0.1,   family = "shifted_lognormal", resp_name = "RT", sigma = 0.3, shift = 200)) bridge <- brms_bridge(spec) #> library(brms) #> fit <- brm( #>   RT ~ effect + (1 + effect | subject) + (1 + effect | item), #>   data   = your_data, #>   family = shifted_lognormal(), #>   prior  = c( #>     prior(normal(0, 2.5), class = "Intercept"), #>     prior(normal(0, 0.5), class = "b", coef = "effect"), #>     prior(normal(0, 1), class = "sd"), #>     prior(lkj(2), class = "cor") #>   ), #>   chains = 4, iter = 4000, warmup = 2000, cores = 4, #>   control = list(adapt_delta = 0.95) #> )  bridge$formula #> [1] "RT ~ effect + (1 + effect | subject) + (1 + effect | item)"

what: Build a design specification from a flat list of design inputs — build_spec
title: Build a design specification from a flat list of design inputs — build_spec
text: Assemble portable design specification (plain list, serialisable [spec_json()]) flat set inputs collected -code application: sample sizes, two-level factor levels, fixed intercept effect, random-effect standard deviations within-subject crossed designs, response family parameters.

title: Build a design specification from a flat list of design inputs — build_spec
code: build_spec(p)

title: Build a design specification from a flat list of design inputs — build_spec
text: p named list design inputs. Common fields `name`, `seed`, `n_subject`, `design_kind` (`""` `"within"`), `include_items`, `n_item`, `factor_name`, `lev1`, `lev2`, `intercept`, `effect`, `family`, `resp_name`, family parameters `sigma`, `shift`, `thresholds`, `phi`; within-design random effects use `subj_int_sd`, `subj_slope_sd`, `subj_corr`, `item_int_sd`, `item_slope_sd`, `item_corr`.

title: Build a design specification from a flat list of design inputs — build_spec
text: design specification nested list, ready [simulate_design()],   [spec_json()], power precision functions.

title: Build a design specification from a flat list of design inputs — build_spec
code: build_spec(list(name = "demo", seed = 1, design_kind = "between",   factor_name = "group", lev1 = "control", lev2 = "treatment", n_subject = 40,   intercept = 100, effect = 5, family = "gaussian", resp_name = "", sigma = 10)) #> $name #> [1] "demo" #>  #> $seed #> [1] 1 #>  #> $units #> $units$subject #> $units$subject$n #> [1] 40 #>  #>  #>  #> $factors #> $factors[[1]] #> $factors[[1]]$name #> [1] "group" #>  #> $factors[[1]]$levels #> [1] "control"   "treatment" #>  #> $factors[[1]]$contrasts #> $factors[[1]]$contrasts$effect #> [1] -0.5  0.5 #>  #>  #> $factors[[1]]$between #> [1] "subject" #>  #>  #>  #> $fixed #> $fixed$intercept #> [1] 100 #>  #> $fixed$coefficients #> $fixed$coefficients$effect #> [1] 5 #>  #>  #>  #> $response #> $response$family #> [1] "gaussian" #>  #> $response$name #> [1] "score" #>  #> $response$sigma #> [1] 10 #>  #> $response$round #> [1] 4 #>  #>

text: conventional response-column name family (example `"RT"`   `"shifted_lognormal"`), `"outcome"` unrecognised family.

code: default_response_name("bernoulli") #> [1] "accuracy"

what: Generate a self-contained, reproducible R script from a specification — generate_r_script
title: Generate a self-contained, reproducible R script from a specification — generate_r_script
text: Embed specification R list literal (via `deparse`, round-trips exactly), returned script reproduces design without external file. turns design built -code application reproducible script; application's Verify button runs script clean R session confirms reproduces data bit--bit.

title: Generate a self-contained, reproducible R script from a specification — generate_r_script
code: generate_r_script(spec)

title: Generate a self-contained, reproducible R script from a specification — generate_r_script
text: spec design specification (list), produced [build_spec()].

title: Generate a self-contained, reproducible R script from a specification — generate_r_script
text: length-one character string containing runnable R script loads `pilotr`,   embeds specification, simulates data.

title: Generate a self-contained, reproducible R script from a specification — generate_r_script
code: spec <- build_spec(list(name = "demo", seed = 1, design_kind = "between",   factor_name = "group", lev1 = "a", lev2 = "b", n_subject = 20,   intercept = 0, effect = 0.5, family = "gaussian", resp_name = "", sigma = 1)) cat(generate_r_script(spec)) #> # Reproducible simulation exported by pilotr. #> # install.packages("pilotr")   # once available; then run this script as-is. #> library(pilotr) #>  #> spec <- list(name = "demo", seed = 1L, units = list(subject = list(n = 20L)),  #>     factors = list(list(name = "group", levels = c("a", "b"),  #>         contrasts = list(effect = c(-0.5, 0.5)), between = "subject")),  #>     fixed = list(intercept = 0, coefficients = list(effect = 0.5)),  #>     response = list(family = "gaussian", name = "score", sigma = 1,  #>         round = 4L)) #>  #> data <- simulate_design(spec)              # analysis-ready data frame #> # write.csv(data, "data.csv", row.names = FALSE) #> # pow  <- power_mixed(spec, n_sims = 200)   # simulation-based power + Type S/M

what: Load a design specification from a JSON file — load_spec
title: Load a design specification from a JSON file — load_spec
text: Load design specification JSON file

title: Load a design specification from a JSON file — load_spec
code: load_spec(path)

title: Load a design specification from a JSON file — load_spec
text: path Path JSON design-specification file.

title: Load a design specification from a JSON file — load_spec
text: specification nested list, sub-lists left unsimplified   structure round-trips exactly. Pass result [simulate_design()].

title: Load a design specification from a JSON file — load_spec
code: spec <- build_spec(list(name = "demo", seed = 1, design_kind = "between",   factor_name = "group", lev1 = "a", lev2 = "b", n_subject = 20,   intercept = 0, effect = 0.5, family = "gaussian", resp_name = "", sigma = 1)) f <- tempfile(fileext = ".json") writeLines(spec_json(spec), f) identical(simulate_design(load_spec(f)), simulate_design(spec)) #> [1] TRUE

what: Create a shared cross-language random-number generator — make_rng
title: Create a shared cross-language random-number generator — make_rng
text: Build combined linear congruential generator (L'Ecuyer 1988) used R Python implementations, given specification seed produce identical data either language. See package `SPEC.md` draw-order contract.

title: Create a shared cross-language random-number generator — make_rng
code: make_rng(seed)

title: Create a shared cross-language random-number generator — make_rng
text: seed single number used seed generator (coerced non-negative integer).

title: Create a shared cross-language random-number generator — make_rng
text: list three functions: `uniform()` returns one standard-uniform draw,   `normal()` returns one standard-normal draw, `normals(k)` returns length-`k`   numeric vector standard-normal draws.

title: Create a shared cross-language random-number generator — make_rng

what: Build the modelling data frame from a simulated data set and its specification — model_data
title: Build the modelling data frame from a simulated data set and its specification — model_data
text: Add analysis response column `.y` (log-transformed lognormal families), numeric contrast columns implied categorical factors, interaction product columns (`:b` coefficient becomes column `a_b`).

title: Build the modelling data frame from a simulated data set and its specification — model_data
code: model_data(spec, d)

title: Build the modelling data frame from a simulated data set and its specification — model_data
text: spec design specification (path list). d simulated data set, returned [simulate_design()].

title: Build the modelling data frame from a simulated data set and its specification — model_data
text: data frame `d` augmented `.y` response column contrast   interaction columns required auto-derived model.

title: Build the modelling data frame from a simulated data set and its specification — model_data
code: spec <- build_spec(list(name = "d", seed = 1, design_kind = "between",   factor_name = "group", lev1 = "a", lev2 = "b", n_subject = 20,   intercept = 0, effect = 0.5, family = "gaussian", resp_name = "", sigma = 1)) head(model_data(spec, simulate_design(spec))) #>   subject group   score      .y effect #> 1       1     a  0.4240  0.4240   -0.5 #> 2       2     a -0.8216 -0.8216   -0.5 #> 3       3     a  0.8925  0.8925   -0.5 #> 4       4     a -1.1222 -1.1222   -0.5 #> 5       5     a -1.3471 -1.3471   -0.5 #> 6       6     a -1.0623 -1.0623   -0.5

what: Derive the lmer formula implied by a specification — model_formula
title: Derive the lmer formula implied by a specification — model_formula
text: Construct mixed-model formula (response `.y`, produced [model_data()]) fixed-effect coefficients random-effects structure specification. Interaction coefficients written `:b` become formula terms `a_b`.

title: Derive the lmer formula implied by a specification — model_formula
code: model_formula(spec)

title: Derive the lmer formula implied by a specification — model_formula
text: spec design specification (path list).

title: Derive the lmer formula implied by a specification — model_formula
text: [stats::formula] object suitable fitting `lme4` `lmerTest`.

title: Derive the lmer formula implied by a specification — model_formula
code: spec <- build_spec(list(name = "d", seed = 1, design_kind = "within",   include_items = TRUE, n_subject = 10, n_item = 8, factor_name = "cond",   lev1 = "a", lev2 = "b", intercept = 6, effect = 0.05,   subj_int_sd = 0.12, subj_slope_sd = 0.04, subj_corr = 0.2,   item_int_sd = 0.08, item_slope_sd = 0.02, item_corr = -0.1,   family = "shifted_lognormal", resp_name = "", sigma = 0.3, shift = 200)) model_formula(spec) #> .y ~ effect + (1 + effect | subject) + (1 + effect | item) #> <environment: 0x55cc32670478>

what: Power curve over sample size for a crossed mixed-effects design — power_curve_mixed
title: Power curve over sample size for a crossed mixed-effects design — power_curve_mixed
text: Sweep number subjects compute mixed-effects power , locate power crosses target. Calls [power_mixed()] requires `lme4` `lmerTest` packages.

title: Power curve over sample size for a crossed mixed-effects design — power_curve_mixed
code: power_curve_mixed(spec, subject_ns, n_sims = 60, alpha = 0.05)

title: Power curve over sample size for a crossed mixed-effects design — power_curve_mixed
text: spec design specification (path list) one within-unit factor. subject_ns numeric vector subject counts evaluate. n_sims Number Monte Carlo replicates per point. alpha Two-sided significance level.

title: Power curve over sample size for a crossed mixed-effects design — power_curve_mixed
text: data frame one row per sample size columns `n_subject`, `power`,   `type_m`.

title: Power curve over sample size for a crossed mixed-effects design — power_curve_mixed
code: # \donttest{ if (requireNamespace("lme4", quietly = TRUE) &&     requireNamespace("lmerTest", quietly = TRUE)) {   spec <- build_spec(list(name = "p", seed = 1, design_kind = "within",     include_items = TRUE, n_subject = 12, n_item = 12, factor_name = "cond",     lev1 = "a", lev2 = "b", intercept = 6, effect = 0.05,     subj_int_sd = 0.12, subj_slope_sd = 0.04, subj_corr = 0.2,     item_int_sd = 0.08, item_slope_sd = 0.02, item_corr = -0.1,     family = "shifted_lognormal", resp_name = "", sigma = 0.3, shift = 200))   power_curve_mixed(spec, subject_ns = c(12, 18), n_sims = 8) } #>   n_subject power   type_m #> 1        12  0.25 2.573080 #> 2        18  0.25 2.489337 # }

what: Simulation-based power and design analysis for a two-group Gaussian design — power_design
title: Simulation-based power and design analysis for a two-group Gaussian design — power_design
text: Estimate power repeatedly simulating specification applying two-sample t-test, alongside Type S (sign) Type M (magnitude) design-analysis errors Gelman Carlin (2014).

title: Simulation-based power and design analysis for a two-group Gaussian design — power_design
code: power_design(spec, n_sims = 1000, alpha = 0.05)

title: Simulation-based power and design analysis for a two-group Gaussian design — power_design
text: spec design specification (path list) two-group Gaussian design. n_sims Number Monte Carlo replicates. alpha Two-sided significance level.

title: Simulation-based power and design analysis for a two-group Gaussian design — power_design
text: list elements `n_sims`, `alpha`, `power`, `n_significant`, `true_effect`,   `mean_estimate`, `type_s` (sign-error rate among significant replicates), `type_m`   (mean exaggeration ratio among significant replicates).

title: Simulation-based power and design analysis for a two-group Gaussian design — power_design
text: Gelman, . Carlin, J. (2014). Beyond Power Calculations: Assessing Type S   (Sign) Type M (Magnitude) Errors. Perspectives Psychological Science,   9(6), 641-651. doi:10.1177/1745691614551642

title: Simulation-based power and design analysis for a two-group Gaussian design — power_design
code: spec <- build_spec(list(name = "d", seed = 1, design_kind = "between",   factor_name = "group", lev1 = "a", lev2 = "b", n_subject = 64,   intercept = 100, effect = 5, family = "gaussian", resp_name = "", sigma = 10)) power_design(spec, n_sims = 50) #> $n_sims #> [1] 50 #>  #> $alpha #> [1] 0.05 #>  #> $power #> [1] 0.6 #>  #> $n_significant #> [1] 30 #>  #> $true_effect #> [1] 5 #>  #> $mean_estimate #> [1] 5.238285 #>  #> $type_s #> [1] 0 #>  #> $type_m #> [1] 1.361989 #>

what: Simulation-based power and design analysis for a crossed mixed-effects design — power_mixed
title: Simulation-based power and design analysis for a crossed mixed-effects design — power_mixed
text: replicate, simulate ground-truth specification, fit maximal model `y ~ cond + (1 + cond | subject) + (1 + cond | item)` `lmerTest`, test fixed effect using Satterthwaite p-values. Reports power together Type S Type M errors Gelman Carlin (2014). Requires `lme4` `lmerTest` packages.

title: Simulation-based power and design analysis for a crossed mixed-effects design — power_mixed
code: power_mixed(spec, n_sims = 100, alpha = 0.05)

title: Simulation-based power and design analysis for a crossed mixed-effects design — power_mixed
text: spec design specification (path list) exactly one within-unit factor. n_sims Number Monte Carlo replicates. alpha Two-sided significance level.

title: Simulation-based power and design analysis for a crossed mixed-effects design — power_mixed
text: list elements `n_sims`, `n_converged`, `alpha`, `power`, `n_significant`,   `true_effect`, `mean_estimate`, `type_s`, `type_m`.

title: Simulation-based power and design analysis for a crossed mixed-effects design — power_mixed
code: # \donttest{ if (requireNamespace("lme4", quietly = TRUE) &&     requireNamespace("lmerTest", quietly = TRUE)) {   spec <- build_spec(list(name = "p", seed = 1, design_kind = "within",     include_items = TRUE, n_subject = 12, n_item = 12, factor_name = "cond",     lev1 = "a", lev2 = "b", intercept = 6, effect = 0.05,     subj_int_sd = 0.12, subj_slope_sd = 0.04, subj_corr = 0.2,     item_int_sd = 0.08, item_slope_sd = 0.02, item_corr = -0.1,     family = "shifted_lognormal", resp_name = "", sigma = 0.3, shift = 200))   power_mixed(spec, n_sims = 10) } #> $n_sims #> [1] 10 #>  #> $n_converged #> [1] 10 #>  #> $alpha #> [1] 0.05 #>  #> $power #> [1] 0.2 #>  #> $n_significant #> [1] 2 #>  #> $true_effect #> [1] 0.05 #>  #> $mean_estimate #> [1] 0.05286316 #>  #> $type_s #> [1] 0 #>  #> $type_m #> [1] 2.57308 #>  # }

what: Precision and ROPE curve over sample size — precision_curve
title: Precision and ROPE curve over sample size — precision_curve
text: Sweep number subjects report ROPE decision probabilities size, identify minimum analysable N focal effect reaches determinate decision target probability (example 0.90). Calls [precision_design()] requires `lme4` package.

title: Precision and ROPE curve over sample size — precision_curve
code: precision_curve(   spec,   focal,   subject_ns,   formula = NULL,   prep = NULL,   rope = 0.05,   n_sims = 60 )

title: Precision and ROPE curve over sample size — precision_curve
text: spec design specification (path list). focal named numeric vector (coefficient name true value) character vector focal coefficient names. subject_ns numeric vector subject counts evaluate. formula Optional `lme4` formula; `NULL` derived via [model_formula()]. prep Optional data-preparation function; `NULL` derived via [model_data()]. rope Half-width region practical equivalence. n_sims Number Monte Carlo replicates per sample size.

title: Precision and ROPE curve over sample size — precision_curve
text: data frame one row per focal effect sample size, adding `n_subject`   column columns returned [precision_design()].

title: Precision and ROPE curve over sample size — precision_curve
code: # \donttest{ if (requireNamespace("lme4", quietly = TRUE)) {   spec <- build_spec(list(name = "pr", seed = 1, design_kind = "within",     include_items = TRUE, n_subject = 12, n_item = 12, factor_name = "cond",     lev1 = "a", lev2 = "b", intercept = 6, effect = 0.05,     subj_int_sd = 0.12, subj_slope_sd = 0.04, subj_corr = 0.2,     item_int_sd = 0.08, item_slope_sd = 0.02, item_corr = -0.1,     family = "shifted_lognormal", resp_name = "", sigma = 0.3, shift = 200))   precision_curve(spec, focal = c(effect = 0.05), subject_ns = c(12, 18), n_sims = 8) } #>   n_subject  param true mean_ci_width p_meaningful p_equivalent n_converged #> 1        12 effect 0.05     0.1642526        0.000            0           8 #> 2        18 effect 0.05     0.1306036        0.125            0           8 # }

what: Precision and ROPE design analysis at a fixed sample size — precision_design
title: Precision and ROPE design analysis at a fixed sample size — precision_design
text: fast frequentist analogue Bayesian highest-density-interval-versus-ROPE design analysis. Across Monte Carlo replicates, fit model record, focal fixed effect, whether 95 equivalence (practically meaningful effect) entirely inside (practical equivalence zero), along expected interval width. Requires `lme4` package.

title: Precision and ROPE design analysis at a fixed sample size — precision_design
code: precision_design(   spec,   focal,   formula = NULL,   prep = NULL,   rope = 0.05,   n_sims = 100 )

title: Precision and ROPE design analysis at a fixed sample size — precision_design
text: spec design specification (path list). focal named numeric vector mapping focal coefficient names true values, character vector coefficient names. formula Optional `lme4` formula; `NULL` derived specification via [model_formula()]. prep Optional function mapping simulated data set modelling data; `NULL` derived via [model_data()], log-transforms outcome builds contrast interaction columns, focal names follow auto-formula (interactions written `a_b`). rope Half-width region practical equivalence; effect `abs(beta) < rope` treated practically equivalent zero. n_sims Number Monte Carlo replicates.

title: Precision and ROPE design analysis at a fixed sample size — precision_design
text: data frame one row per focal effect columns `param`, `true`,   `mean_ci_width`, `p_meaningful`, `p_equivalent`, `n_converged`.

title: Precision and ROPE design analysis at a fixed sample size — precision_design
code: # \donttest{ if (requireNamespace("lme4", quietly = TRUE)) {   spec <- build_spec(list(name = "pr", seed = 1, design_kind = "within",     include_items = TRUE, n_subject = 12, n_item = 12, factor_name = "cond",     lev1 = "a", lev2 = "b", intercept = 6, effect = 0.05,     subj_int_sd = 0.12, subj_slope_sd = 0.04, subj_corr = 0.2,     item_int_sd = 0.08, item_slope_sd = 0.02, item_corr = -0.1,     family = "shifted_lognormal", resp_name = "", sigma = 0.3, shift = 200))   precision_design(spec, focal = c(effect = 0.05), rope = 0.02, n_sims = 10) } #>    param true mean_ci_width p_meaningful p_equivalent n_converged #> 1 effect 0.05     0.1698034          0.1            0          10 # }

what: Launch the pilotr no-code app. — run_app
title: Launch the pilotr no-code app. — run_app
text: Runs bundled Shiny app locally. package installed, app calls package's functions directly. Running locally gives user private R process, work blocked shared process, power simulations can parallelised across user's cores.

title: Launch the pilotr no-code app. — run_app
code: run_app(..., async = NULL)

title: Launch the pilotr no-code app. — run_app
text: ... passed [shiny::runApp()] (e.g. `port`, `launch.browser`). async TRUE (default 'future' 'promises' installed), set multisession future plan power runs execute background worker block UI. relevant case app deployed shared multi-user instance.

title: Launch the pilotr no-code app. — run_app
text: return value; called side effect launching Shiny application,   blocks R session app closed.

title: Launch the pilotr no-code app. — run_app
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ run_app() } # }

what: Simulate a data set from a design specification — simulate_design
title: Simulate a data set from a design specification — simulate_design
text: Generate analysis-ready data set portable design specification: linear predictor built fixed effect sizes (categorical contrasts, continuous predictors, interactions) plus crossed -subject -item random intercepts slopes, mapped chosen response family.

title: Simulate a data set from a design specification — simulate_design
code: simulate_design(spec)

title: Simulate a data set from a design specification — simulate_design
text: spec design specification, given either path JSON file already-parsed list (example [build_spec()] [load_spec()]).

title: Simulate a data set from a design specification — simulate_design
text: data frame one row per observation, containing `subject` column,   optional `item` column, grouping, factor, continuous-predictor columns,   response column named specification.

title: Simulate a data set from a design specification — simulate_design
code: spec <- build_spec(list(name = "demo", seed = 1, design_kind = "between",   factor_name = "group", lev1 = "control", lev2 = "treatment", n_subject = 40,   intercept = 100, effect = 5, family = "gaussian", resp_name = "", sigma = 10)) head(simulate_design(spec)) #>   subject   group    score #> 1       1 control 104.2395 #> 2       2 control  91.7843 #> 3       3 control 108.9245 #> 4       4 control  88.7779 #> 5       5 control  86.5289 #> 6       6 control  89.3767

what: Serialise a design specification to pretty-printed JSON — spec_json
title: Serialise a design specification to pretty-printed JSON — spec_json
text: Serialise design specification pretty-printed JSON

title: Serialise a design specification to pretty-printed JSON — spec_json
code: spec_json(spec)

title: Serialise a design specification to pretty-printed JSON — spec_json
text: spec design specification (list), produced [build_spec()].

title: Serialise a design specification to pretty-printed JSON — spec_json
text: length-one character string containing specification pretty-printed JSON,   portable artefact R 'Python' packages consume.

title: Serialise a design specification to pretty-printed JSON — spec_json
code: spec <- build_spec(list(name = "demo", seed = 1, design_kind = "between",   factor_name = "group", lev1 = "a", lev2 = "b", n_subject = 20,   intercept = 0, effect = 0.5, family = "gaussian", resp_name = "", sigma = 1)) cat(spec_json(spec)) #> { #>   "name": "demo", #>   "seed": 1, #>   "units": { #>     "subject": { #>       "n": 20 #>     } #>   }, #>   "factors": [ #>     { #>       "name": "group", #>       "levels": ["a", "b"], #>       "contrasts": { #>         "effect": [-0.5, 0.5] #>       }, #>       "between": "subject" #>     } #>   ], #>   "fixed": { #>     "intercept": 0, #>     "coefficients": { #>       "effect": 0.5 #>     } #>   }, #>   "response": { #>     "family": "gaussian", #>     "name": "score", #>     "sigma": 1, #>     "round": 4 #>   } #> }

title: pilotr 0.1.0
text: Initial release. Generative simulation experimental behavioural data portable JSON design specification shared Python package name, using shared cross-language random-number generator bit-identical output. Response families: Gaussian, shifted lognormal, Bernoulli, Poisson, ordinal Beta. Simulation-based power precision/ROPE design analysis, reporting power alongside Type S Type M errors Gelman Carlin (2014). -code Shiny application specification, launched run_app().
